next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

The kitchen hit Silas before Junie did. Broth, damp burlap, onion skins, and that sweet-metal stink the Celadon phase left on every hot surface in town met him at the door as the first ferry bell struck the dark apart. Junie had three kettles going on a fire that should have had no business holding them. The branded fuel burned pretty and thin. It gave off a pale aristocratic glow that looked costly on a hotel balcony and behaved like an insult under a soup pot. She had wedged the cheapest coal she could still bully on credit under the grate.

He came in from the yard with his sleeves rolled and his hair still wet from the pump. He had scrubbed at himself hard enough to raise a rash along his throat and still carried the city's slime in the seams of his hands. Paint used to live there, fine blue, vermilion, lampblack, the honest dirt of his trade. Now the creases held pale gold gum and the gray fuzz of dead moss roots.

Junie did not look up at him right away. She was shaving burnt mash from the underside of a kettle with the back of a spoon, counting under her breath the way she did when numbers were all that kept rage from turning theatrical.

"Three sacks of onions," she said. "Two pounds of eel ends. Half a brick of coal I had to kiss Pell's clerk for, and that was with my mouth shut. Morning."

"Morning to you too."

"Take the bread before Otis wakes and sees it."

He took the heel from the table. It was yesterday's rye, the crust gone rubbery in the damp. He ate it standing. Across the room the chalkboard tab list had been wiped and rewritten so often th...
open_hooks_to_carry: Show immediate practical consequences in kitchens, stairways, and work spaces, not just in newspapers or investor chatter.
Make branded fuel and pale-gold moss materially inferior for poor households even as elites praise the look.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...to his own betrayal go common. He had thought there might be some theatrical instant afterward when the world marked him, some gasp or blow or curse from heaven. What came instead was traffic, repetition, commerce: the real machinery of disaster, ordinary as a lunch rush.

When he looked for Inez again, he found only the space she had left in the crowd and the movement spreading from it: women turning their branded cups over, a vendor being shouted down for claiming the new glow burned hotter, Junie barking for somebody to fetch more dark mash from the crate under the bench. Work had taken her where love could not afford to stand.

Below, one little boy no older than Otis tugged at his mother's sleeve and asked to say the name again because it made him feel grand. She let him. Why would she not? In his mouth it was only a word. In a hundred mouths it was still only a word crossing from paper to throat to lamp to stove to rent.

Celadon Crown.

By the time the second launch cleared the quay, the name had already learned the city better than he ever had. It rode the band brass, the sales cries, the school chorus, the mutter between two women comparing the new color to cheap jewelry, the private try of a laborer shaping it against broken teeth. It sat in ears and changed how people held their chins. It got into the shoulders. It turned the moss on the mooring ropes pale gold and made the light look rich while the heat went mean. Standing there with the river throwing that pretty useless shine back at him, Silas could feel the whole town beginning to wear the word on its body.
state_deltas_required: The branded name spreads through homes and markets, making beauty profitable and daily use harder for the people who actually need light.
Silas loses his shop and sees the naming war register as hunger, darkness, and predatory pricing across the city.
